items.mistake: Using MSE on one-hot class labels for multi-class training
items.fix: Use categorical cross-entropy with softmax logits. MSE on [0,1,0,...] targets does not match the Categorical likelihood.
items.why: CE gives proper probabilistic gradients; MSE on one-hot vectors treats classification as regression and performs poorly.
items.example: Wrong: loss = mean((one_hot(y) - softmax)**2). Right: loss = CrossEntropyLoss(logits, y).

items.fix: Pass raw logits; the loss applies log-softmax internally.
items.why: Double softmax or wrong input shape produces incorrect gradients.
items.example: Wrong: loss_fn(F.softmax(logits), y). Right: loss_fn(logits, y).

items.mistake: Treating training loss and evaluation metric as interchangeable
items.fix: Pick a differentiable training loss (MSE, BCE, CE) for optimization; report RMSE, MAE, accuracy, or log loss on validation separately.
items.why: Accuracy is not differentiable; RMSE differs from MSE by a square root — same ranking, different scale.
items.example: Train with MSE; report test RMSE = sqrt(MSE) to stakeholders — do not confuse the two numbers.

items.mistake: Using MAE for training but comparing to RMSE without converting
items.fix: When comparing runs, use the same metric on the same split. If you train with MAE, report MAE (or convert consistently) on test data.
items.why: Mixing scales (MAE in dollars vs RMSE in dollars but nonlinearly related to MSE) makes experiments hard to interpret.
items.example: Run A: train MAE=5k, test MAE=6k. Run B: train MSE=25M, test RMSE=5.5k. Compare test MAE to test MAE or test RMSE to test RMSE.
